metadata.summary: Behavioral-interview framework using STAR format. Every question must use past-tense framing, never hypothetical. Hypothetical answers lack predictive validity.

content.title: STAR Interview Method
content.summary: The STAR framework (Situation-Task-Action-Result) is a behavioral-interview method for extracting evidence of past performance and structuring post-interview analysis. Every STAR question must use past-tense behavioral framing ("Tell me about a time...") — never hypothetical framing ("How would you..."); hypothetical answers have near-zero predictive validity and must be blocked with a prescribed probe.

why.p: McDaniel et al. (1994) meta-analysis shows structured behavioral interviews are ~2x more predictive of job performance than unstructured interviews. The STAR frame forces candidates to produce observable, verifiable evidence rather than values statements.
Pronoun-shift detection ("we" vs. "I") and mandatory result quantification close the two most common inflation paths (team re-attribution and "R" fabrication).

list.item: Behavioral interview rounds for any role where past behavior is the dominant performance predictor: managerial, cross-functional, customer-facing roles.
Calibrating an interviewer team that drifts into hypothetical questions.
Generating tailored STAR question banks per competency from a JD or leveling rubric.
Post-interview transcript analysis: STAR-component tagging and missing-component flagging.
Coaching internal candidates preparing for promotion panels.
Pure technical screens where the signal lives in code, system design, or work samples — STAR adds noise, not signal.
Roles with fewer than 2 years of experience: candidates lack a STAR repertoire; use situational + work-sample combinations.
Cultures where narrative storytelling style is unfamiliar without controlling for it in scoring (some EU/EE/JP contexts).
Crisis or urgency screens where only real-time problem-solving is a valid signal.

rule.statement: Every STAR question must use past-tense behavioral framing: "Tell me about a time..." Never use hypothetical framing ("How would you..."). Hypothetical answers have near-zero predictive validity.
rule.rationale: McDaniel et al. (1994) meta-analysis: structured behavioral interviews are ~2x more predictive than unstructured. The behavioral framing is what produces the predictive signal; hypotheticals do not.

list.item: Situation: the context or background — "Tell me about a time when..."
Task: the specific challenge or responsibility — "What was your role/responsibility?"
Action: what the candidate specifically did — "What did YOU do?" (not the team)
Result: the outcome and what they learned — "What was the result? Can you quantify it?"

section.name: interview-flow
section.code:
1. ASK: "Tell me about a time when you had to deal with a difficult stakeholder."
2. LISTEN for Situation: - Specific context (project, team, timeframe) - Who was involved
3. PROBE for Task if missing: "What was your specific responsibility?" "What were you trying to accomplish?"
4. DIG INTO Action: "Walk me through exactly what you did." "What was YOUR role, not the team's?"
5. CLOSE on Result: "What was the outcome?" "What metrics can you share?" "What would you do differently?"

example.note: Complete STAR: all four components present, personal contribution clear, result quantified.
example.code: "When X happened (S), I was responsible for Y (T), so I did A, B, C (A), resulting in 30% improvement in Z (R)."

example.note: Missing Action: no personal contribution visible.
example.code: "The project was struggling, so we fixed it together as a team."
"I led a team through a challenging period and we worked really hard."
"I would approach this by first understanding all stakeholders' needs, then building consensus around the solution."

list.item: "Tell me about a time you led a team through a significant change." → Probe: "What resistance did you encounter and how did you handle it?"
"Describe a situation where you had to motivate an underperforming team member." → Probe: "What was the outcome for that person 3 months later?"
"Give an example of when you made an unpopular decision." → Probe: "What would you do differently in hindsight?"
"Tell me about a time you had to delegate an important task." → Probe: "How did you ensure quality without micromanaging?"
"Describe a complex problem you solved at work." → Probe: "Walk me through your decision-making process at the hardest moment."
"Tell me about a time you had to make a decision with incomplete information." → Probe: "What would have changed if you had waited for more data?"
"Give an example of when you identified a problem before others noticed." → Probe: "How did you validate that it was actually a problem?"
"Describe a time you had to troubleshoot under pressure." → Probe: "How did you manage your own stress while solving it?"
"Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult news to stakeholders." → Probe: "What was their reaction and how did you manage it?"
"Describe a situation where you had to persuade someone who disagreed." → Probe: "What argument ultimately changed their position?"
"Give an example of when you had to explain something complex simply." → Probe: "How did you know they truly understood?"
"Tell me about a miscommunication and how you resolved it." → Probe: "What systemic change did you make to prevent recurrence?"
"Describe a successful cross-functional project you contributed to." → Probe: "What was YOUR specific contribution vs. the team's?"
"Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a colleague." → Probe: "How is your relationship with that person today?"
"Give an example of when you had to work with someone whose style differed from yours." → Probe: "What did you adapt and what did you hold firm on?"
"Tell me about a time you had to adapt to a major change at work." → Probe: "What was the hardest part of the adjustment?"
"Describe a situation where priorities suddenly shifted." → Probe: "What did you do about work you had already committed to?"
"Tell me about a time you failed and how you responded." → Probe: "What specifically would you do differently?"

rule.statement: Block hypothetical answers. When a candidate answers with "I would..." trigger the probe: "Can you give me a specific past example of when you did this?" Do not score hypothetical answers; mark as NOT_STAR.
rule.rationale: Hypothetical answers have near-zero predictive validity per behavioral psychology research. Accepting them inflates scores for candidates who are good at reasoning about what they should do, not what they actually did.

rule.statement: Flag pronoun shifts. When an answer uses "we" throughout with no first-person Action statement, trigger the probe: "What did YOU personally do in this situation?" Mark answers as "pronoun_we_only" in transcript analysis.
rule.rationale: Team re-attribution is the most common STAR inflation path. Candidates may legitimately use "we" for shared work but must identify their specific contribution before the Action is scoreable.

rule.statement: Always close on Result. For any answer that describes actions without an outcome, use the mandatory probe: "What was the end result?" An answer without R is incomplete regardless of S/T/A quality.
rule.rationale: Result quantification is the primary defense against coaching-site rehearsed STAR answers. Specific numbers (30% reduction, $2M saved) are harder to fabricate and easier to verify in reference checks.

rule.statement: Agents must not assign numeric scores to candidate STAR responses. Agents may extract component spans, flag missing components, and quote verbatim evidence. Numeric scores are always human-assigned.
rule.rationale: LLM scoring rewards fluency and polish, systematically disadvantaging non-native speakers and neurodiverse candidates. The legal liability of automated employment scoring without bias audit is substantial.

rule.statement: Cap at one competency per question. Reject any double-barreled question ("Tell me about a time you led a team AND resolved conflict"). One question, one competency, 3-5 questions per 45-minute round.
rule.rationale: Double-barreled questions allow candidates to answer only the part they are strong on, producing incomplete evidence for both competencies simultaneously.

rule.statement: Weight question generation explicitly to avoid leadership/communication clustering. Force at least one question per round covering execution, technical depth, or delivery under constraint.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to leadership and communication questions; execution and technical depth are under-covered by default, producing STAR banks that miss critical signals for IC roles.

rule.statement: Send candidates a 1-page STAR prep brief before the interview loop. This raises the floor for non-native speakers without raising the ceiling for native-speaker candidates.
rule.rationale: STAR storytelling style disadvantages candidates unfamiliar with the format. Pre-briefing reduces communication-style bias without compromising the competency signal.

antipattern.description: Live-coaching prompts sent to candidates during a real interview.
antipattern.reason: Coaching a candidate mid-interview is equivalent to fraud — it eliminates the comparative basis for the evaluation and creates legal exposure.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: Rehearsed STAR answers from coaching sites are common; without probing, the "R" is fabricated. Force quantified results + a "who else was involved I could verify with" follow-up.
Candidates re-attribute team accomplishments as personal "A". The agent transcript-scorer will accept it unless explicitly prompted to flag pronoun shifts ("we" → "I").
"R" inflation: 30% gains, 10x improvements, etc. Without a baseline question, the result is undebatable.
STAR rewards verbal articulation, not necessarily competence. Cross-check with work samples.
Agent-generated STAR questions cluster on leadership/communication; weak coverage of execution and depth.
Single-component STAR (just Action) is common in technical contributors — scoring as "incomplete" under-rates strong builders.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive STAR as a four-stage pipeline owned by faion-recruiter-agent. Stage 1 (question bank generation, sonnet) consumes the competency table and emits 3-5 STAR questions per competency, weighted by importance. Stage 2 (live-interview prompt support, sonnet, low latency) is optional — feeds the interviewer suggested probes when the candidate's answer is missing a component (S/T/A/R parser). Stage 3 (post-interview transcript STAR-extraction, sonnet) tags S/T/A/R spans and missing components per question. Stage 4 (cross-candidate calibration, opus) compares STAR completeness + evidence quality across the loop, never assigns scores. Scoring is always human.

list.item: Always start with "Tell me about a time when..." — never "How would you handle...". The latter is hypothetical, predictive validity is near zero.
Probe Action, not Result, first. Strong candidates self-quantify; weak ones can't even when prompted.
Force pronouns in the Action: "What did *you* do versus the team?" Re-attribution catches inflation.
Ask "what would you do differently" — gates metacognition, often more diagnostic than the original Result.
Cap to 3-4 STAR questions per 45-minute round. More creates rushed shallow stories.
Send candidates a 1-page STAR prep brief before the loop. It reduces non-native-speaker disadvantage and lifts the *floor*, not the ceiling.
When scoring, separate "STAR completeness" from "competency demonstrated". A complete STAR can still show weak competency.
Keep a per-question "strong / borderline / weak" exemplar pack for new interviewer calibration. Refresh quarterly.
For panels, assign each interviewer 1-2 competencies; do not ask all interviewers all STAR questions.

template.purpose: Per-question S/T/A/R component scoring sheet with competency evidence and hire recommendation.
Before/during/after interview checklist for hiring managers using STAR.
Deterministic STAR completeness checker: flags missing components and 'we'-only answers before LLM analysis.
